title: WiFi RC Car
subtitle: Build a Car You Drive from Your Phone — Over WiFi!
overview: In this session, students build a fully functional WiFi-controlled RC car using an ESP8266, an L298N motor driver, and two DC motors. A web page served by the ESP8266 displays direction buttons — Forward, Backward, Left, Right, Stop — that control the car from any browser on the same network. Students experience the complete integration of electronics, motor control, WiFi communication, and web development in one exciting project.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — RC car chassis, L298N wiring, ESP8266 on car, browser control page, car in motion.

keyTopics: DC Motor — An electric motor that converts electrical energy into rotational motion; speed and direction controlled by voltage polarity
H-Bridge — A circuit arrangement that allows current to flow through a motor in either direction, enabling forward and reverse rotation
L298N Motor Driver — A dual H-bridge IC module that controls two DC motors independently, supporting direction and PWM speed control
IN1, IN2, IN3, IN4 — Direction control pins on the L298N; HIGH/LOW combinations determine each motor's rotation direction
ENA, ENB — Enable pins on the L298N; PWM signals here control motor speed (0–255); jumper = full speed always
PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) — Rapidly switching power on and off to simulate variable voltage; controls motor speed on the ESP8266
2WD Chassis — A two-wheeled drive robot car platform; left and right motors controlled independently for turning
Differential Steering — Turning is achieved by running one motor faster or slower than the other (or reversing one) — no steering servo needed
ESP8266 Web Server — Hosts the HTML control page; parses URL paths (/forward, /backward, /left, /right, /stop) to determine motor commands
URL Path Parsing — Reading the HTTP request to extract the command path: `if (request.indexOf('/forward') != -1)` triggers forward motion
Power Supply — Motors need more current than the ESP8266 can provide; a separate battery pack (4× AA or Li-ion) powers the L298N motor supply
Free Wheeling / Coasting vs Active Braking — Stopping motors by removing power (coast) vs actively braking by setting both IN pins HIGH

resources.title: Random Nerd Tutorials — ESP8266 WiFi Car

activity.title: Build and Drive a WiFi RC Car
parts: L298N dual H-bridge motor driver module
2WD robot car chassis with 2 DC motors and wheels
4× AA battery holder (or 7.4V Li-ion pack) for motor power
Power bank or USB cable for ESP8266 power
Laptop with Arduino IDE and ESP8266WiFi library
Smartphone or laptop browser for control
steps: Step 1 — Chassis Assembly: Attach the two DC motors to the chassis, fit the wheels, and secure the caster wheel at the front. Mount the battery holder underneath

Step 2 — L298N Wiring: Connect motor A wires to L298N OUT1/OUT2. Connect motor B wires to OUT3/OUT4. Connect the battery pack positive to L298N 12V input and negative to GND. Connect L298N GND to ESP8266 GND (shared ground)
Step 3 — ESP8266 to L298N Control Pins: Connect IN1 → D1, IN2 → D2, IN3 → D3, IN4 → D4. Leave ENA and ENB jumpers on for full speed (or connect to D5/D6 for PWM speed control)
Step 4 — Motor Test: Write a simple sketch — no WiFi yet. Test each motor direction by setting IN1/IN2 and IN3/IN4 HIGH/LOW combinations. Confirm both motors spin correctly in both directions

Step 5 — WiFi Web Server: Add WiFi connection code. Create a web server on port 80. Build an HTML page with 5 styled buttons: Forward (▲), Backward (▼), Left (◄), Right (►), Stop (■). Each button links to a URL path: /forward, /backward, /left, /right, /stop

Step 6 — URL Path Parsing: In the server loop, read the HTTP request string. Use `indexOf('/forward')`, `indexOf('/left')` etc. to detect which button was pressed and call the corresponding motor function

Step 7 — Motor Control Functions: Write functions — `moveForward()`: both motors forward. `moveBackward()`: both motors reverse. `turnLeft()`: right motor forward, left motor stop/reverse. `turnRight()`: left motor forward, right motor stop/reverse. `stopCar()`: all IN pins LOW
Step 8 — Full Drive Test: Upload the sketch. Open Serial Monitor, copy the IP address. Open it in a phone browser. Drive the car around the classroom!

Challenge: Add PWM speed control — a Slow/Fast toggle button on the web page that uses analogWrite() on ENA/ENB to switch between 50% and 100% speed. Also add a horn button that beeps a buzzer mounted on the car

quiz.q: What does a DC Motor do?
options: Converts electrical energy into light
Converts electrical energy into rotational motion — it spins when current flows through it
Converts rotational motion into electrical energy
Controls the speed of other motors
quiz.explanation: DC Motor = electricity → spinning! When current flows through the motor coils, it creates a magnetic field that makes the axle rotate. Reverse the current direction and the motor spins the other way. This is how the RC car moves forward and backward.

quiz.q: What is an H-Bridge circuit and why is it needed for DC motor control?
options: A bridge that connects the ESP8266 to the motor directly
A circuit arrangement that allows current to flow through a motor in either direction, enabling both forward and reverse rotation
A type of power regulator that steps down voltage for the motor
A wireless communication bridge between the phone and the car
quiz.explanation: H-Bridge is the direction switcher! A DC motor needs current reversed to spin the other way, but a microcontroller can only output HIGH or LOW — not reverse voltage. The H-Bridge uses four switches arranged in an H shape to control which direction current flows through the motor.

quiz.q: What is the role of the L298N module in the RC car project?
options: It provides WiFi connectivity to the car
It is a dual H-bridge motor driver that controls the speed and direction of both DC motors based on signals from the ESP8266
It measures the car's speed using a sensor
It stores the web page HTML for the control interface
quiz.explanation: L298N is the muscle! The ESP8266 sends low-current control signals (IN1–IN4) to the L298N. The L298N uses its own power supply to drive the high-current motors. Without it, the motors would drain too much current and damage the ESP8266's GPIO pins.

quiz.q: On the L298N, what do the IN1 and IN2 pins control?
options: The power supply voltage to the module
The direction of Motor A — different HIGH/LOW combinations on IN1 and IN2 make the motor spin forward, reverse, or stop
The PWM speed of both motors simultaneously
The WiFi connection strength
quiz.explanation: IN1 and IN2 control Motor A direction! IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW → forward. IN1=LOW, IN2=HIGH → reverse. IN1=LOW, IN2=LOW → stop (coast). IN3 and IN4 do the same for Motor B. This is how the ESP8266 tells the L298N which way to spin each wheel.

quiz.q: What is Differential Steering in a 2WD robot car?
options: Using a steering servo to turn the front wheels left or right
Turning by running one motor faster or slower than the other — or reversing one motor — without needing a separate steering mechanism
Measuring the speed difference between the two wheels with encoders
A gear differential that splits torque between left and right wheels
quiz.explanation: Differential steering = tank-style turning! To turn left: slow down or reverse the left motor while the right motor runs forward. The car pivots around its left wheel. This is simpler and more compact than a steering servo — no extra hardware needed, just motor speed difference.

quiz.q: Why does the L298N need a SEPARATE battery pack rather than using the ESP8266's power supply?
options: The L298N only works with AC power
DC motors draw significantly more current than the ESP8266 can supply — a separate battery prevents voltage drop that would crash or damage the ESP8266
The ESP8266 and L298N use different communication protocols
Separate power is only needed when using PWM speed control
quiz.explanation: Motors are current hungry! DC motors can draw 200–600mA or more, especially under load. The ESP8266's 3.3V regulator can only supply ~300mA total for everything. Separate motor power prevents the motor's current draw from browning out the ESP8266 and causing random resets or WiFi drops.

quiz.q: What is PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) used for in motor control?
options: Sending WiFi data packets to the browser
Rapidly switching power on and off to simulate variable voltage, controlling motor speed between 0% and 100%
Measuring the rotation speed of the motor shaft
Protecting the motor from overheating
quiz.explanation: PWM = simulated dimmer switch for motors! Instead of true variable voltage, PWM rapidly pulses power on and off. At 50% duty cycle (on half the time), the motor gets effectively half the voltage and spins at half speed. `analogWrite(ENA, 128)` sets 50% speed; `analogWrite(ENA, 255)` = full speed.

quiz.q: How does the ESP8266 web server know which direction button was pressed on the control page?
options: Each button sends a Bluetooth signal to the ESP8266
Each button links to a different URL path (/forward, /left, etc.) — the ESP8266 reads the HTTP request and checks which path was requested using indexOf()
The browser sends a JSON command to the ESP8266's API
The buttons directly control GPIO pins via the browser's JavaScript
quiz.explanation: URL path parsing! Each HTML button is a link to a path: `Forward`. When clicked, the browser sends `GET /forward HTTP/1.1` to the ESP8266. The code checks `if (request.indexOf('/forward') != -1)` and triggers the forward motor function. Simple and effective!

quiz.q: What must be shared between the ESP8266 and the L298N for the circuit to work correctly?
options: The same WiFi network
A common GND (ground) connection — without shared ground, the control signals from ESP8266 to L298N have no reference and the motors behave unpredictably
The same power supply voltage
The same baud rate in Serial Monitor
quiz.explanation: Shared ground is essential! In any multi-power-supply circuit, all GND connections must be joined together. The ESP8266's GND and the L298N's GND must be connected. Without it, the IN1–IN4 signals from the ESP8266 have no return path and the L298N won't respond correctly.

quiz.q: To make the RC car turn LEFT using differential steering, what should the motors do?
options: Both motors stop completely
Both motors run forward at full speed
The right motor runs forward while the left motor stops or reverses — causing the car to pivot left
The left motor runs forward while the right motor stops or reverses
quiz.explanation: Turn left = right motor drives, left motor brakes/reverses! The car pivots around the slower or reversed side. Right motor forward + left motor stopped = gentle left curve. Right motor forward + left motor reverse = sharp on-the-spot left spin. Same principle as a tank!

quiz.q: What is the ENA pin on the L298N used for?
options: Enabling the ESP8266's WiFi antenna
An enable pin for Motor A — connecting it to a PWM signal controls Motor A's speed; leaving the jumper on sets it to full speed
Connecting the external power supply
Enabling serial communication with the ESP8266
quiz.explanation: ENA = Motor A's throttle! With the jumper on, Motor A always runs at full power when IN1/IN2 are set. Remove the jumper and connect ENA to an ESP8266 PWM pin — then `analogWrite(ENA, speed)` controls how fast Motor A spins. ENB does the same for Motor B.

quiz.q: A student's RC car moves but can only go forward and cannot reverse. What is the most likely cause?
options: The WiFi connection is too slow
The IN1 and IN2 wires for one or both motors are swapped or the reverse function sets both IN pins to LOW (coast) instead of reversing HIGH/LOW
The L298N needs a firmware update
The ESP8266 ran out of memory
quiz.explanation: Check the reverse function! Common mistakes: reverse() sets IN1=LOW, IN2=LOW (coast/stop) instead of IN1=LOW, IN2=HIGH (reverse). Or motor wires are swapped on the L298N outputs so 'reverse' command actually sends no signal. Test each motor independently with a simple test sketch.

quiz.q: Why is it important to test each motor direction separately BEFORE assembling the full web server code?
options: To check that the WiFi SSID is correct
To verify the wiring and motor directions are correct early — catching wiring mistakes before adding complexity makes debugging far easier
The motor test is required by the L298N library
To calibrate the speed of both motors to exactly the same value
quiz.explanation: Always test components step by step! If you wire everything and upload the full code at once, a wiring mistake causes confusing behaviour. By testing motors first with a simple sketch — no WiFi, no web server — you isolate and fix hardware issues before adding software complexity.

quiz.q: What real-world robots and vehicles use the same WiFi web server control principle as this RC car?
options: Commercial aircraft autopilot systems
WiFi-controlled warehouse robots, telepresence robots, drone ground control stations, and home surveillance robots that stream video and receive movement commands over a network
Traditional infrared TV remote controls
Bluetooth-controlled toys with a dedicated remote
quiz.explanation: This project mirrors real robotics! Warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva), telepresence robots (for remote meetings), and even some Mars rover concepts use network-based command and control. The principle — send a command over a network, robot executes it — scales from your RC car to robots on other planets!

quiz.q: What upgrade would allow the RC car to be driven from ANYWHERE in the world, not just on the same WiFi network?
options: Replacing the L298N with a more powerful motor driver
Integrating Blynk IoT — replacing the local web server with Blynk cloud so the control app works over the internet from any location
Adding a GPS module to the car
Increasing the PWM frequency for the motors
quiz.explanation: Blynk makes it global! The local web server only works when phone and ESP8266 are on the same WiFi. With Blynk, button presses in the app travel through the cloud to the ESP8266 anywhere with internet. Add a Joystick widget in Blynk for smooth directional control — a natural upgrade from this project!
